message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>While the pathogenesis of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) is becoming increasingly clear, there is little data on IgA response, the first line of bronchial immune defense.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To determine, whether COVID-19 is associated with a vigorous total IgA response and whether IgA autoantibodies are associated with complications of severe illness. Since thrombotic events are frequent in severe COVID-19 and resemble hypercoagulation of antiphospholipid syndrome (APS), our approach focused on antiphospholipid antibodies (aPL).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Materials and methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In this retrospective cohort study we compared clinical data and aPL from 64 patients with COVID-19 from three independent centers (two in Switzerland, one in Liechtenstein). Samples were collected from April 9, 2020 to May 1, 2020. Total IgA and aPL were measured with FDA-approved commercially available clinical diagnostic kits.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Clinical records of the 64 patients with COVID-19 were reviewed and divided into a cohort with mild illness (mCOVID,
                    <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                    =26 [41%]), a discovery cohort with severe illness (sdCOVD,
                    <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                    =14 [22%]) and a confirmation cohort with severe illness (scCOVID,
                    <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                    =24 [38%]). Severe illness was significantly associated with increased total IgA (sdCOVID,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0.01; scCOVID,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.001). Total IgG levels were similar in both cohorts. Among aPL, both cohorts with severe illness significantly correlated with elevated anti-Cardiolipin IgA (sdCOVID and scCOVID,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.001), anti-Cardiolipin IgM (sdCOVID,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0.003; scCOVID,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.001), and anti-Beta2 Glycoprotein-1 IgA (sdCOVID and scCOVID,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.001). Systemic lupus erythematosus was excluded from all patients as a potential confounder of APS.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Higher total IgA and IgA-aPL were consistently associated with severe illness. These novel data strongly suggest that a vigorous antiviral IgA-response triggered in the bronchial mucosa induces systemic autoimmunity.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Severe COVID-19 is associated with elevated serum IgA and antiphospholipid IgA-antibodies
